Through her labour in the cooperative large-scale enterprise the woman will be placed economically on equal footing with the man and will attain the same share in the cooperative as he; she will be his free comrade, liberated (emancipated) not merely from the bondage of the house, but also from that of capital. Freely disposing of herself, equal to the man, she will put an end to every kind of prostitution, the lawful as well as the unlawful, and for the first time in world history raise monogamy, applying equally to man and woman, into an institution that really exists, not merely according to the letter.</p>

```{=html}
<p>These are not utopian proposals, but scientific convictions, grounded in definite facts. Whoever wants to refute them must prove that these presuppositions do not exist. Since this has not so far succeeded, there of course remains to the gentlemen and ladies who want to know nothing of this development no other "refutation" than to become morally indignant and to place their morality in the most favourable light through lies and falsifications. With this, however, they will not hold back the inevitable development by a single minute.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It stands firm that it is not through social democracy or the essence of socialist production that the dissolution of the traditional family form is brought about, but through the economic development that has been proceeding before our eyes for decades already. The socialist society will not and cannot hold it back, but it will remove from the consequences of the development all those tormenting and degrading aspects that necessarily accompany it in the capitalist society. And while the latter leads to the dissolution of every family bond, of every ordered marriage, in the socialist society the dissolution of the present-day family form takes place only to the extent that it is displaced by a higher one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>That is the truth about social democracy striving for the abolition of marriage and the family.<br/> <a id="ch4-t8" name="ch4-t8"> </a></p>
```

## 8. The Confiscation of Property

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Our opponents, who know better than we ourselves what we want and who are able to depict the “state of the future” with greater precision than we can, have furthermore discovered that Social Democracy will be unable to inaugurate its rule in any way other than through the expropriation (dispossession) of artisans and peasants, from whom everything is to be confiscated (taken without compensation) — not merely house and farm, but also the dispensable movables and — the savings-bank deposits. Alongside the alleged violent tearing apart of all family ties, this is one of the chief trump cards played against us.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To this it must be remarked that the essence of a socialist society in no way requires such a confiscation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The Social Democratic programme says nothing about confiscation. Not out of timidity, so as not to give offence, but simply because nothing definite can be said about it. One can state with certainty only that the tendency of economic development makes it necessary that the <em>large-scale enterprises</em> pass into social property and be operated on behalf of society. In what manner this transition takes place, whether the inevitable expropriation becomes a confiscation or a redemption, whether it proceeds peacefully or by force — these are questions to which no one can give an answer. Nor does an appeal to the past help much here. The transition can take place in the most varied manner, just as the abolition of feudal burdens took place in the most varied manner in the different countries. The manner of the transition depends on the general situation in which it takes place, on the strength and insight of each of the classes that come into consideration, and so forth — all circumstances that cannot be calculated in advance. In historical development the unexpected plays the greatest role.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is quite self-evident that the Social Democrats wish that the expropriation of the large-scale enterprises, which has become inevitable, should proceed as painlessly as possible, peacefully and with universal consent. But historical development is determined just as little by our wishes as by those of our opponents.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In no case can it be said that the implementation of the Social Democratic programme demands under all circumstances that the property whose expropriation has become necessary be confiscated.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What can be said with certainty, however, is that economic development makes necessary only the expropriation of a part of the existing property. What it demands is cooperative property in the <em>means of production;</em> the private property in the means of <em>personal consumption</em> is in no way affected thereby. This applies not merely to foodstuffs, furniture, and so on. Let us recall what was said in the previous chapter about the savings banks. They are a means of placing the possessions of the non-capitalist classes at the disposal of the capitalists. Each of the small savings deposits is by itself too insignificant to run a capitalist enterprise. Only through their combination are they capable of fulfilling the functions of a capital. To the extent that the capitalist enterprises pass into the property of society, the possibility of investing the savings-bank deposits at interest will diminish. They will cease to be capital and will become mere <em>uninterest-bearing hoard,</em> consumption funds. But that is surely something quite different from the <em>confiscation</em> of the savings deposits.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But such a confiscation is not only unnecessary for <em>economic</em> reasons, it is also quite improbable for <em>political</em> reasons. For the small savings deposits derive for the most part from the exploited classes, those whose strength alone can introduce socialism into society. Only one who holds these classes to be absolutely incapable of judgement will believe that, in order to get the means of production into their hands, they would begin by robbing themselves of their own nest eggs!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the transition to socialist production not only does not require the expropriation of the means of consumption, it also does not require the expropriation of all owners of <em>means of production.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is the <em>large-scale enterprise</em> that makes socialist society necessary. Cooperative production also requires cooperative property in the means of production. But just as private property in the means of production stands in contradiction to cooperative labour in the large-scale enterprise, so cooperative or social property in the means of production is in contradiction to the small-scale enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The latter requires, as we have seen, private property in the means of production. The abolition of that property for the small-scale enterprises would be all the more pointless, since the very tendency of socialism is to place the workers in possession of the necessary means of production. For the small-scale enterprises the expropriation of the means of production would therefore amount to taking them from their present owners in order to give them back to them — a senseless procedure.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p><em>The transition to socialist society therefore in no way requires the expropriation of the small artisans and small peasants.</em> This transition will not only take nothing from them, it may rather bring them certain advantages. For since socialist society brings with it the tendency to replace commodity production with production for direct consumption, as we have seen, it must also strive to transform all payments to society, the taxes or the possible interest on the mortgages that have passed into social property — insofar as these are not abolished altogether — from <em>money payments</em> into payments in <em>kind</em> — grain, wine, cattle, and so on. But this means an enormous relief for the peasants. They already strive for it in many places today. But it is impossible under the rule of commodity production. Only socialist society can bring it and thereby remove a chief cause of the ruin of the peasant economy.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is the <em>capitalists</em> who in fact expropriate peasants and artisans, as we have seen. <em>Socialist society puts an end to this expropriation.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To be sure, <em>economic development</em> is not halted by socialism. On the contrary, socialism is the only means of making its continuation beyond a certain measure possible. As in present-day society, so too in the socialist one the large-scale enterprise will develop more and more and absorb the small-scale enterprises more and more. But here too what was said of the family and marriage applies. The direction of development remains the same, but socialism removes all the hideous and painful phenomena by which, in present-day society, the course of development is accompanied, while it lets its advantages benefit all.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Today the transformation of the peasant or artisan from a worker in a small-scale enterprise into a worker in a large-scale enterprise means his transformation from a <em>property-holder</em> into a <em>proletarian.</em> In a socialist society, on the other hand, the peasant or artisan who passes over to work in a cooperative large-scale enterprise becomes a participant in all the advantages of the large-scale enterprise; his position improves considerably; his transition from the small-scale enterprise to the large-scale enterprise is no longer to be compared with the transformation of a property-holder into a proletarian, but rather with that of one who possesses little into one who possesses much.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p><em>The small-scale enterprise is irretrievably doomed to destruction. But only social democracy makes it possible for the peasants and artisans in their entirety to become workers of the large-scale enterprise without sinking into the proletariat.</em> Only in a socialist society does the dissolution of peasant agriculture and of the handicraft, which has become unavoidable, signify an improvement in the situation of the peasants and artisans.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The driving force of economic development will no longer be <em>competition,</em> which eliminates the backward enterprises and expropriates their owners, but rather the <em>attractive power</em> which the more highly developed enterprises and forms of enterprise exert upon the workers of the backward enterprises and forms of enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This kind of development is not only painless, it also proceeds much more rapidly than that brought about by competition. Today, when the introduction of new, higher forms of enterprise cannot take place without the expropriation of owners of backward enterprises, without the privations and sufferings of great masses of workers who become superfluous, every economic advance meets with stubborn resistance. We have seen how tenaciously the producers in many cases still cling to the most outlived forms of production, how desperately they hold fast to them so long as there is but a breath of strength in them. Never has a mode of production been so revolutionary as the present one, never has a mode of production within a single century caused such gigantic upheavals in all fields of human activity, and yet how many ruins of obsolete, outlived forms of production have still survived!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>As soon as the fear of being hurled into the proletariat through the giving up of an independent enterprise disappears, as soon as the advantages of the social large-scale enterprise make themselves felt for all those involved in the most diverse fields, as soon as everyone is offered the possibility of sharing in the enjoyment of these advantages, only fools will still strive to preserve obsolete forms of enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What capitalist large-scale industry has not managed to accomplish in a century, the socialist large-scale enterprise will achieve within a short time: the absorption of the backward small-scale enterprises. It will achieve this without expropriation, through the attractive power of the more advantageous enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Where peasant production is not yet commodity production but predominantly production for one's own use, peasant agriculture may perhaps continue to exist for some time in socialist society. Ultimately, even in these circles people will learn to understand the advantages of the social large-scale enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The transition from the small-scale enterprise to the large-scale enterprise in agriculture will be accelerated and facilitated by the progressive abolition of the opposition between town and country, by the tendency towards the transfer of industry to the open countryside, which must necessarily be characteristic of socialist society. We must unfortunately content ourselves here with this indication, since a more detailed exposition would lead too far afield.<br/> <a id="ch4-t9" name="ch4-t9"> </a></p>
```

## 9. The Distribution of Products in the 'State of the Future'

```{=html}
<p class="fst">We wish to consider only <em>one</em> further point regarding the 'state of the future', the one that appears to be the most important of all. The first question that outsiders address to a socialist is generally this: 'How will you carry out the division of your wealth? Shall each receive an equal amount and each the same?'</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Division! This is what sticks in the philistine's bones. In division his entire conception of socialism is exhausted.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is not long ago that in Germany even the most educated people assumed that the communists wanted to distribute all the riches present in the nation among the people.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>That this notion could persist so stubbornly despite all protests on the part of Social Democracy is probably not to be blamed on the malice of our opponents alone, but rather, and perhaps to the greater part, on their inability to understand the conditions created by the development of large-scale industry. Their intellectual horizon still in many respects does not extend beyond the conceptions that correspond to small-scale enterprise. From the standpoint of small-scale enterprise, however, division is the only possible form of a kind of socialism. Division indeed lies very close to the petty bourgeois and the peasant. Since the emergence of commodity production in antiquity, it has occurred countless times, whenever a few families – merchants or landowners – had accumulated great riches and brought artisans or peasants into dependence and distress, that the latter sought to help themselves by driving out the rich and distributing their property. Only a hundred years ago, in the French Revolution, which so sharply emphasised the right of private property, artisans and peasants carried out division – for example, of the church estates. <em>Division, that is the socialism of small-scale enterprise, the socialism of the 'conservative', 'state-preserving' strata of the people, not the socialism of the large-industrial proletariat.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It has taken a long time, but in the end it has after all succeeded in hammering into even the thinker-skulls of the German nation the recognition that the Social Democrats do not want to <em>divide</em>, but rather strive for the opposite of this, the <em>combination</em> of the means of production hitherto distributed among several owners into the hands of society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But with this the question of division is not disposed of. If the means of production belong to society, then naturally the disposal over the products created with the help of these means of production also falls to it. In what manner will it distribute these among its members? By the measure of equality or according to the measure of the labour performed by each? And will, in the latter case, every kind of labour receive the same remuneration, regardless of whether it is pleasant or unpleasant, difficult or easy, whether it requires prior knowledge or not?</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The answering of this question seems to be the crux of socialism. Not only do our opponents ride on it most eagerly; the earlier socialists themselves devoted the greatest attention to the question of the distribution of products. From Fourier to Weitling and from the latter to Bellamy there runs a series of the most manifold attempts at a solution, which often bear witness to admirable acuteness. There is no lack of 'positive proposals'; many of them are as simple as they are practical.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The question, however, by no means has the importance that is so often ascribed to it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Formerly people were accustomed to regard the distribution of products as something quite independent of production. And since the contradictions and grievances of the capitalist mode of production came to light first of all in the manner peculiar to it of distributing products, it was quite natural that the exploited and their friends saw in the 'unjust' distribution of products the root of all evil.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Naturally they assumed, in accordance with the views that prevailed at the beginning of our century, that this distribution was a consequence of the prevailing <em>ideas</em>, of the conceptions of law. In order to eliminate the unjust distribution, it was therefore a matter of devising a better and more just one and of convincing the world of its advantages. The just distribution could be none other than the opposite of the existing one. Today the crassest <em>inequality</em> prevails; the principle of distribution, according to some, should therefore be <em>equality</em>. Today the idler sits in riches, the worker suffers want, and therefore others called out: <em>To each according to his achievements</em> (or in more recent form: <em>To each the yield of his labour</em>). But against the one formula as against the other objections were raised, and so a third arose: <em>To each according to his needs.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Since then the socialists have come to recognise that the distribution of products in a society is determined not by the conceptions of law and forms of law prevailing within it, but by the mode of production prevailing within it. The share of landowners, capitalists and wage labourers in the total product in present-day society is determined by the role that land and soil, the objects of capital ownership, and labour-power play in the present-day mode of production. In a socialist society, to be sure, the distribution of products will not be carried out according to blindly operating laws which assert themselves without properly coming to the consciousness of those involved. Just as today in a large industrial enterprise production and wage payment are well-considered and ordered according to a plan, so this too will have to be the case in a socialist society, which is nothing but a single gigantic industrial enterprise. The rules according to which the distribution of products is carried out will be established by those involved themselves. But it will not lie in their discretion which rules they wish to establish; these will not be able to be devised arbitrarily for the sake of this or that 'principle', but will be determined by the actual conditions which prevail in society, above all by the conditions of <em>production</em>.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus, for example, the respective level of the productivity of labour will be of the greatest influence on the manner of distributing the product of labour. One can imagine that the application of science in industry will one day yield so high a productivity of labour that people possess everything they need in abundance; then the formula: 'To each according to his needs' would come to be carried out without difficulty, almost of itself. On the other hand, the deepest conviction of the justice of this formula would not be able to bring about its implementation if the productivity of labour remained so slight that one does not produce, without excessive expenditure of labour, more than one just needs.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The formula: 'To each the yield of his labour' will in any case founder on the needs of production. For if this formula is to have any sense, then it presupposes that the entire respective product of social labour is distributed to the members of the socialist commonwealth. This view, just like that of the great division with which the reign of socialism is supposedly to be inaugurated, moves within the circle of ideas of present-day private property. To distribute all products year in, year out would mean gradually restoring private property in the means of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The nature of socialist production necessarily entails that only a <em>fraction</em> of the products manufactured at any given time comes up for distribution. All those products which are destined for the continuation and expansion of <em>production</em> (as well as for covering any deficits) naturally do not come up for distribution. Nor do those products which serve <em>common consumption,</em> that is, the establishment, maintenance, or expansion of public institutions for education, instruction, healing, recreation, amusement, and the like.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The number and extent of such institutions is already steadily growing in present-day society; in these fields too the large-scale enterprise increasingly pushes back the small-scale enterprise – here in particular the family. In a socialist society this development will naturally not be checked, but rather promoted.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The number of products that can pass into private consumption, into private property, will therefore be much smaller in a socialist society <em>in relation to the quantity of the total product</em> than in present-day society, where almost all products are commodities, private property. It is not, as nowadays, almost the entire product that comes up for distribution, but only a remainder.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But even over this remainder the socialist society will not be able to dispose entirely at will; in its distribution too the needs of the continuation of production will be decisive. And since production will be in a state of constant transformation and development, for this reason alone the forms and kinds of distribution of products in a socialist society will be subject to manifold changes.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is entirely utopian thinking to suppose that the task is to devise a particular system of distribution which is then to be authoritative for all eternity. In this field too, as in all others, the socialist society will make no leap, but will build upon what it finds already in existence. The distribution of goods in a socialist society will, for the foreseeable future, probably proceed only in forms which represent a further development of the <em>wage forms</em> existing today. From these it will have to set out. And just as the forms of wages vary not only over time but also simultaneously for different branches of labour and in different regions, so it is by no means excluded that in a socialist society, according to the various historical traditions and the habits that persist in the population, and according to the changing needs of production, the most manifold forms of distribution of products will occur side by side. One must picture a socialist society neither as something rigid nor as something uniform, but as something caught up in the full flow of development and endowed with that wealth of changing forms which follows with natural necessity from the increase of the division of labour, of world commerce, and of the rule of science and art in society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Besides “sharing out,” it is “equality” that causes our opponents the greatest headaches. “The Social Democrats,” they say, “want everyone to receive an equal share of the total product. Thus the diligent worker is to receive just as much as the lazy one; heavy and unpleasant work is to be rewarded just as well as light and pleasant work; the simple manual task just as well as the most artistic creation that requires years of preparation, and so forth. Naturally, under these circumstances everyone will work as little as possible, no one will perform the difficult and unpleasant work, no one will want to learn anything more, and the end result will be the complete disruption of society, barbarism. From this one clearly perceives the impracticability of the Social Democratic aspirations.”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>How nonsensical this assertion is scarcely needs to be discussed in detail after what has been said. We are not so far-sighted as our opponents, and can therefore by no means declare with the same certainty as they whether the “state of the future” will decree the full equality of all incomes or not. But should it ever occur to a socialist society to issue such a regulation, and should this regulation really begin to draw after it the appalling consequences so clearly seen by our opponents, then the natural consequence would be that not socialist production but – the principle of equality would be thrown overboard.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Our opponents would have a right to infer the impracticability of socialist society from the equality of incomes only if they succeeded in proving, first, that this equality is under all circumstances incompatible with the progress of production. This proof they have not so far furnished, and never will be able to furnish, since the activity of the individual in production depends not merely on his remuneration, but on the most various circumstances, such as a sense of duty, ambition, emulation, habit, the attractive force of the work, and so forth, about whose configuration in the future society we can have only conjectures, but no certainty – conjectures, incidentally, that speak <em>against</em> and not <em>for</em> the view of our opponents; but the opponents would in addition also have to prove, secondly, that equality of incomes is necessarily conditioned by the nature of a socialist society, so that such a society would be inconceivable without this equality. This proof too they cannot possibly furnish, for even a glance at the various forms of communist production which have existed hitherto, from the most primitive communism of the earliest savages down to the mark associations and household associations of our peasants, shows how manifold are the forms of distribution of products that are compatible with common ownership of the means of production. All the forms of present-day wage payment – fixed salary, time-wage, piece-wage, premiums for performances that exceed a certain average measure, differing payment for differing activities – all these wage forms are – of course suitably transformed – compatible with the nature of a socialist society, and each of them may, according to the various needs and habits of the members of society and the needs of production, play at times a more or less large role in the various socialist societies.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>By this, however, it is not meant to be asserted that the principle of <em>equality</em> (which need not necessarily be uniformity) of incomes or of the material conditions of life will not also play a role in socialist societies, yet not as the goal of a forcible <em>levelling</em> imposed without further ado and abruptly, but as the goal of a <em>natural development,</em> as a <em>tendency.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the capitalist mode of production there prevails both the tendency towards the enlargement and the tendency towards the diminution of the differences in incomes, towards the <em>enlargement</em> and towards the <em>reduction</em> of <em>inequality.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>By dissolving the middle strata and causing the great capitals to swell ever more, it visibly widens the gulf that exists between the <em>mass</em> of the population and its <em>summits</em>. These tower ever higher above the former, ever more unapproachable and abrupt. At the same time, however, the tendency of the capitalist mode of production goes towards equalising, more and more, the existing income differences within the mass of the population itself. It not only casts peasants and petty bourgeois into the proletariat or presses their incomes down to a proletarian level; it also increasingly abolishes the differences existing within the bosom of the proletariat. The machine strives more and more to abolish the differences which longer or shorter apprenticeship, greater or lesser supply of labour-power, tighter or looser organisation under the dominance of the handicraft and even of the manufacture produced in the wages of the various strata of workers, and which, in view of the permanence in the forms of production that existed before the introduction of machinery, became standing, firmly rooted differences. Today the differences in the levels of wages of the various strata of workers are ceaselessly changing and more and more equalising themselves. At the same time, too, as we have seen, the income of the head-workers begins to become more and more equal to that of the proletarians: this equalisation, which our opponents brand with the greatest moral indignation as an intention of Social Democracy, is taking place before their eyes in present-day society!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In socialist society, of course, all those tendencies towards the increase of inequality that spring from private property in the means of production come to an end. On the other hand, the tendency towards the equalisation of the differences in incomes will come to expression there all the more strongly. But here again what we were able to remark above applies, when we spoke of the dissolution of the traditional form of the family and then of the demise of the small-scale enterprise: the <em>tendency</em> of economic development remains in a certain respect the same in socialist as in capitalist society, but it expresses itself in another manner. Today the equalisation of the incomes of the great mass of the population takes place by way of the higher incomes being <em>pressed down</em> to the level of the low ones. In a socialist society it will naturally come about in such a way that the lower incomes are <em>raised</em> and made equal to the higher ones.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Our opponents seek to frighten the workers and petty bourgeois by saying that an equalisation of the incomes would only worsen their situation, for the total income of the well-to-do classes would not suffice, if distributed among them, to bring the income of the worst-placed classes up to the level of the average income of the working class. In the interest of "equality" the better-placed workers and petty bourgeois would therefore also have to give up something of their income. They would lose through socialism, instead of gaining.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>There is this much truth in that: the most wretched, above all the lumpenproletarians, are indeed today so numerous and their distress so great that it would scarcely suffice to distribute the enormous incomes of the rich among them in order to make possible for them the existence of a better-placed worker. Whether that is precisely a reason which makes the preservation of our splendid society particularly urgently necessary appears to us doubtful, however. We should think that even an <em>improvement</em> of the distress that would be brought about by such a distribution would have to signify a step forward.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But, as we know, it is not a question of "sharing out" at all, but of the change of the <em>mode of production. The transition from capitalist to socialist production, however, must unconditionally bring about a rapid upward surge in the mass of products produced annually.</em> Let us not forget that capitalist commodity production has become an obstacle to economic development, an obstacle to the full unfolding of the productive forces of modern society. It is not only incapable of absorbing the small-scale enterprises to the degree that technical development makes possible, indeed requires; it also no longer even has the possibility of employing all the available labour-power. It squanders it by driving an ever-growing fraction of it into the ranks of the unemployed, of the lumpenproletarians, of the parasites, of the unproductive middlemen, and by feeding another fraction in the standing armies without benefit to production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A socialist society would find productive work for all this labour-power; it would considerably increase the number of workers active in production, perhaps double it, but in the same measure also cause the total mass of the annual products to swell. This expansion of production alone would already suffice to raise the incomes of all the workers, not merely those of the most wretched among them.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But through the transition to a socialist production, as we have already set out, the absorption of the small-scale enterprises and their replacement by large-scale enterprises would also be greatly promoted, and thereby the productivity of labour in general considerably heightened. It would be possible not merely to raise the incomes of the workers, but also to reduce the working time.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is accordingly quite senseless to assert that socialism means the equality of the beggar's sack for all. This equality is not the tendency of socialism, but that of the present-day mode of production. The transition to socialist production must, by natural necessity, bring with it a raising of the prosperity of <em>all the working classes,</em> including the peasants and the petty bourgeois. According to the circumstances and the economic conditions under which this transition takes place, this raising will be a greater or a smaller one, but in any case a <em>noticeable</em> one. And every further economic advance will from then on bring about an <em>increase,</em> not, as nowadays, a <em>diminution</em> of the general prosperity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This reversal in the direction of income development appears to us even more important for the well-being of society than the absolute increase of incomes. For the thinking human being lives more in the future than in the present; what the former threatens or promises him occupies him more than the enjoyment of the moment. Not <em>being,</em> but <em>becoming,</em> not <em>conditions,</em> but <em>tendencies</em> decide over the happiness and unhappiness of the individual and of whole societies.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We here come to know a new side of the superiority of a socialist society over the capitalist one. It offers not merely <em>heightened prosperity,</em> but also <em>security of existence,</em> a security such as the greatest wealth cannot grant today. If the increase of prosperity affects only the hitherto exploited, then the securing of the livelihood is a welcome gift also for the present-day exploiters, for those whose comfortable living no longer needs an increase, is often no longer capable of one. Insecurity hovers over the rich as over the poor, and it is perhaps even more tormenting than want; it makes even those feel want in spirit who have not yet been seized by it; it is a spectre that does not spare even palaces.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>All investigators who have come to know communist communities — such as Indian or Russian village communes (before their dissolution by commodity production, state intervention, the money economy and the usury that followed from it) or peasant household cooperatives, such as are still found today among the South Slavs — have above all been struck by the sense of calm, of security, of equanimity that was peculiar to all their members. Entirely independent of all the fluctuations of the commodity market, in full possession of their means of production, they are self-sufficient, they regulate their work according to their needs and know beforehand what they have to expect.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>And yet the security which these primitive communist societies offered was not complete. Their mastery over nature was slight, the communities themselves were small. Losses through cattle plagues, crop failures, floods and the like occurred not infrequently and then struck the whole cooperative. How much more secure, by contrast, stands a socialist community of the extent of a modern state, which has at its disposal all the achievements of present-day science!<br/> <a id="ch4-t10" name="ch4-t10"> </a></p>
```

## 10. Socialism and Freedom

```{=html}
<p class="fst">That a socialist society offers its members prosperity and security has been recognised and acknowledged even by many of our opponents. But, they object, these advantages are bought too dearly, for they are paid for with the complete loss of <em>freedom.</em> The bird in the cage may count each day on sufficient feed; it is secured against hunger, storms and enemies. But it lacks freedom, and for that reason it is nonetheless a pitiable creature that yearns to go out into the world of dangers and want, out into the struggle for existence.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Socialism, they say, destroys economic freedom, the freedom of labour. It introduces a despotism compared with which the most unbounded political absolutism is a state of freedom, for the latter captures only <em>one</em> side of the human being, whereas the former captures the <em>whole</em> human being.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>So great is the fear of the bondage of communism that there are even socialists who have been seized by it, the <em>anarchists.</em> These abhor communism as much as commodity production and seek to escape the dangers of both by – wanting both at the same time. They want a communism with commodity production. This is absurd in theory; in practice it amounts to the workers' cooperatives of liberal “self-help” already characterised.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is correct when it is said that socialist production is incompatible with the full freedom of labour, that is, with the freedom of the worker to work when, where and how he pleases. But this freedom of the worker is incompatible with <em>any</em> planned cooperation of several persons, in whatever form it may take place, whether on a capitalist or a cooperative basis. The freedom of labour is possible only for the <em>small-scale enterprise,</em> and even for it only up to a certain degree. Even where the small-scale enterprise is freed from all constricting regulations – compulsory field rotation, guild compulsion and the like – the individual worker still remains dependent on natural and social influences: the peasant, for example, on the weather, the artisan on the state of the market, and so forth. Yet the small-scale enterprise does offer the possibility of a certain freedom of labour; this freedom is its ideal, the most revolutionary ideal of which the petty bourgeois is capable, who cannot see beyond the horizon of the small-scale enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A hundred years ago, at the time of the French Revolution, this ideal was still well grounded in the economic conditions. Today it has lost all support and can only haunt the heads of people who do not see what economic upheaval has taken place since then. With the downfall of the small-scale enterprise, the downfall of the freedom of labour is also necessarily bound up. It is not the Social Democrats who destroy it, but the unstoppable advances of large-scale industry. Precisely those who carry the phrase about the necessity of the freedom of labour most in their mouths, the capitalists, are the ones who contribute most to abolishing it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Not only for factory work does the freedom of labour cease, but for every kind of work in which the individual acts merely as a member of a great whole. It does not exist for the detail workers of the manufactory and of the domestic industry, but neither for all those <em>brain-workers</em> who are active as employees in institutions and not as individuals on their own account. The hospital doctor as well as the schoolteacher, the railway official as well as the newspaper writer, and so forth – none of them has the freedom of labour, but rather they are bound to definite rules, must work at definite, prescribed places at definite times, and so on. And since, as already noted, in the fields of intellectual activity the large-scale enterprise displaces the small-scale enterprise just as in other fields of human endeavour, the freedom of labour is also diminishing more and more for the brain-workers already in present-day society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To be sure, the worker under the rule of capitalist large-scale industry still possesses a certain freedom. If the work in one enterprise does not suit him today, he is free to seek work in another; he can pass from one employment into another. In a socialist commonwealth all the means of production are united in <em>one</em> hand; there is only a single “employer,” whom it is impossible to change.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In this respect, then, the present-day wage labourer has one freedom over the worker of the socialist society, but this freedom cannot really be called the freedom of labour. He may pass ever so often from one factory into another; he will find the freedom of labour in none of them; in each the tasks of every individual worker will be precisely determined and regulated. This is a technical necessity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The freedom whose loss threatens the worker in socialist production is thus not the freedom of labour, but only the freedom to choose his master himself. This freedom is by no means without significance today; it forms a bulwark for the worker, as everyone knows who was or is employed in a monopoly enterprise. But even this freedom is called more and more into question by the economic development; growing unemployment brings it about that the number of positions becoming vacant is far smaller than the number of applicants for them. As a rule the unemployed worker must be glad to find any post at all. And the increasing concentration of the means of production in few hands works towards the result that in the end the worker finds in every enterprise the same “employer,” or at least the same conditions of work.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What our opponents proclaim as the wicked intention of a Social Democracy hostile to culture and freedom is a naturally necessary tendency of the economic development in present-day society. This holds true here, as in so many other fields.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is not Social Democracy, but the economic development, by which the freedom to choose the opportunity for work is abolished just as much as the freedom during work. Social Democracy, to be sure, does not want to, and indeed cannot at all, arrest this development; but, as we have already seen in other fields, so here too it will give the development another shape, one more favourable for the workers. It cannot abolish the dependence of the worker on the economic mechanism in which he forms a little cog, but in place of the dependence of the worker on a capitalist whose interests stand hostilely opposed to his own, it substitutes his dependence on a society of which he himself is a member, a society of comrades with equal rights who have the <em>same interests</em>.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Such a dependence may appear unbearable to a liberal lawyer or man of letters; it is not so for a modern proletarian, as a glance at the <em>trade-union movement</em> shows. The trade unions already give us a picture of that “tyranny of the socialist compulsory state” of which our opponents prattle. There the conditions of work of the individual are already regulated in the most precise and strictest manner, yet it has as yet occurred to no member of one of these societies to see in this an unbearable infringement of his personal freedom. Those who found it necessary to defend the “freedom of labour” against this “terrorism” – often by force of arms, amid bloodshed – were not the workers, but their exploiters. Poor freedom, which today finds no defenders other than the slaveholders!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But in a socialist commonwealth the unfreedom of labour not only loses its oppressive character, <em>it will also become the foundation of the highest freedom</em> that has hitherto been possible for the human race.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This sounds contradictory. But the contradiction is only apparent.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Until the advent of large-scale industry, the labour required to produce and acquire the products necessary for the maintenance of life—let us say, in short, <em>gainful labour</em>—fully absorbed those engaged in it; it demanded the exertion of all one's powers, not merely of the body but also of the mind. This holds not only for the hunter and the fisherman; it holds also for the peasant, the artisan, and the merchant: the life of the gainfully employed person was almost entirely taken up by his gainful activity. It was labour that steeled his sinews and nerves, that made his brain inventive and eager for knowledge. But the more the division of labour developed, the more one-sided it inevitably made the gainfully employed person. Mind and body cease to be active in the most varied fields and to develop all their faculties. Entirely absorbed by the momentary partial task, the gainfully employed lost their sense for the totality of the phenomena of their surroundings. A harmonious, all-round development of the mental and physical powers, an intensive occupation with the questions concerning the interconnections in society and in nature, philosophical thought—that is, a search for the highest truths for their own sake—could under these circumstances be found only among those who remained free of gainful labour. Until the rise of the machine system, this was possible only through shifting this labour onto others, through <em>exploitation.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The most idealistic, the most philosophical race that history has hitherto known, a uniquely outstanding society of thinkers and artists who served science and art for their own sake, was the Athenian aristocracy, was the slaveholding landowners of Athens.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Labour—not only slave labour, but also <em>free</em> labour—was regarded among them as something degrading, and rightly so. It was no arrogance when Socrates said: “The shopkeepers and artisans (banausics) lack education, if only because of the lack of leisure, without which a good education is impossible. <em>They learn only what their trade requires;</em> knowledge in itself has no attraction for them. Thus they occupy themselves with arithmetic only for the sake of trade, not in order to become acquainted with the nature of numbers. They have not the power to strive for anything higher. The tradesman says: the joy in honour and in learning is, in comparison with monetary gain, worthless. The blacksmiths, carpenters, and cobblers may be skilled in their craft, but most of them are slave souls; they do not know what is beautiful, good, and just.”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Since then economic development has advanced, the division of labour has reached an incredible height, and the overgrowth of commodity production has driven the exploiters and the educated into the circle of the gainfully employed. Like the artisans and peasants, today the rich too are wholly absorbed in their gainful activity. It is not in gymnasia and academies that they gather, but at stock exchanges and markets; the speculations in which they are immersed concern not the concepts of truth and justice, but wool and schnaps, Russian loans and Portuguese coupons. Their mental powers are exhausted in these speculations. After the “work” is done, they retain only the energy and interest for the most mindless amusements possible.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>For the educated, however, education has become a commodity, as we have seen. They too no longer have the time or the impulse for the selfless search for truth, for striving after the ideal. Everyone buries himself in his speciality and regards every minute as lost that he spends learning something he cannot turn to account. Hence the present-day craze to remove Greek and Latin from the secondary schools. Here it is less pedagogical grounds that speak against them than the endeavour to let the young learn only what they will one day “need”—that is, what they can convert into money.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Among the men of science as well as of art too, the sense for the whole, the striving after all-round, harmonious development is being lost. Everywhere only one-sided specialised study. Science and art sink to the level of a craft. For them too holds what Socrates said of the banausic occupations. The philosophical spirit is in the process of dying out—that is to say, within the classes mentioned here.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the meantime, however, a new kind of labour has arisen, <em>labour at the machine,</em> and a new class, the <em>proletariat.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The machine robs labour of all mental content. The worker at the machine no longer has to think, no longer has to reflect; he has to obey the machine without a will of his own. It prescribes to him what he has to do; he becomes its appendage.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What holds for labour at the machine holds also, though as a rule to a lesser degree, for the partial labours of the manufactory and of the domestic industry. The breaking down of the labour of the artisan, who creates something whole, into a series of partial labours, each of which, by means of one or several quite simple manipulations, produces only the part of a whole product, forms, as is well known, the preliminary stage and introduction to the machine system.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The first consequence that the monotony and mindlessness of labour brings for the proletarian is the apparent deadening of his mind.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the next consequence is that he feels himself driven to revolt against the excessively long extension of labour. For him, working is not synonymous with living. Life begins for him only when the work is over. For that worker to whom work and life are one, the <em>freedom of labour</em> can mean a <em>free life.</em> The worker who lives only when he is not working can attain a <em>free life</em> only through <em>liberation from</em> <em>labour.</em> Naturally the striving of the latter class of workers cannot be to rid themselves of all labour. Labour is, after all, the precondition of life. But their striving must of natural necessity be to restrict the labour they have to perform so far that it leaves them room for living.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This is one of the strongest roots of the struggle for the shortening of the working time of the modern proletarians, a struggle which the peasants and artisans of the old stamp confront without understanding. It is not a struggle for small economic advantages, for some wage increase, for a reduction in the number of the unemployed; these aims play their part, but at bottom it is a <em>struggle for life.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Yet a further consequence arises from the fact that labour has been stripped of its intellectual content by the machine: the mental powers of the proletarian are not, like those of the other gainfully employed, exhausted by their gainful activity; they lie fallow during it. All the more powerful, therefore, is the proletarian's urge to exercise his mind outside of work, provided the latter grants him even some room for it. One of the most striking phenomena of present-day society is the <em>proletariat's thirst for knowledge.</em> While all other classes seek to kill their leisure time as mindlessly as possible, the proletariat strives with a veritable greed after education. Only one who has had occasion to work among proletarians can fully gauge the strength of this urge for knowledge and enlightenment. But even the more distant observer can get an inkling of it if he compares the newspapers, journals and pamphlets of the workers with the literature that is popular in other circles of society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>And this urge for knowledge is an entirely disinterested one. To the worker at the machine, knowledge cannot help to increase his income. When he seeks the truth, he seeks it for its own sake, not for the sake of any material gain. That is why he does not confine himself to a single, narrower field; his gaze is directed at the whole; he wants to comprehend the whole of society, the whole world. The most difficult riddles attract him the most; with predilection he turns to questions of philosophy, of metaphysics, and it is often hard to bring him back down from the clouds to the earth.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is not the <em>possession</em> of knowledge, but the <em>striving</em> after knowledge that makes the philosopher. It is in the despised, ignorant proletarians that the philosophical spirit of the brilliant thinkers of the Athenian aristocracy revives. But a free development of this spirit is not possible in present-day society. For the proletarians are without the means to instruct themselves, without guidance for systematic study, left to all the accidents and obstacles of an unplanned self-education, and above all without sufficient leisure! Science and art remain for them a promised land, which they behold from afar, for the possession of which they struggle, but which they cannot enter.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only the victory of socialism unlocks for the proletariat all the sources of education; only the victory of socialism makes it possible to shorten the time of labour needed to secure a livelihood so far that the worker is given the leisure necessary to acquire a sufficient stock of knowledge. The capitalist mode of production awakens the proletariat's thirst for knowledge; the socialist mode of production alone can slake it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Not the <em>freedom of labour,</em> but the <em>liberation from labour,</em> as the machine system makes possible to a far-reaching degree in a socialist society, will bring mankind the <em>freedom of life,</em> the <em>freedom of artistic and scientific activity,</em> the <em>freedom of the noblest enjoyment.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>That happy, harmonious cultivation which has hitherto appeared only once in world history, as the privilege of a small band of select aristocrats, will become the common possession of all civilised peoples; what the slaves accomplished for the former, the machines will accomplish for the latter; they will feel all the elevating influences of liberation from gainful labour, without having to accept into the bargain any of those degrading effects through which the slave economy ultimately enervated the aristocrats of Athens. And just as the present-day aids of science and art are far superior to those known two thousand years ago; and just as the present-day world of culture far surpasses little Greece, so will the socialist society far surpass the most brilliant commonwealth that history has hitherto known, in moral greatness and material welfare.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Happy is everyone to whom it is granted to devote his strength to the struggle for the realisation of this glorious ideal!</p>
```

## Notes

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ch4-f8" id="ch4-n8" name="ch4-n8">8.</a> Let it merely be remarked here in passing that, since economic development can by no means come to a standstill under socialist social conditions, its progress will bring about a steady growth in the size that a socialist cooperative requires in order to be able to prosper. That the individual socialist nations will ultimately merge into a single commonwealth, that the whole human race will form a single society, is our firm conviction. However, we are here concerned only with the starting points, not with the later course of the development of socialist social forms, and thus we need not draw the world republic into the scope of our discussion.</p>
```


---
